sources.notes: Repeatedly adjust duplicate drone heights/values and answer when all frequencies become small enough.
Official tutorial observes monotonicity of the maximum frequency and binary-searches time.
Maspy describes simulating only the frequency histogram and batching t steps from the right.

annotation.summary: Find the minimum number of adjustment steps until every value frequency is at most k.
annotation.constraints: The value range and number of operations are large, so direct step simulation is too slow.
annotation.core_idea: The process depends only on the frequency histogram, not on individual identities. If a value has frequency greater than k, the excess units move to the right; the maximum frequency is monotone non-increasing with time. Binary search the answer t, and for a fixed t simulate the batched movement greedily from right to left: excess either lands in a zero-frequency slot within t distance, or shifts exactly t positions right.
annotation.complexity: O(V log answer) with coordinate/value compression.
tricks: simulate frequency histogram instead of elements
batch t steps from right to left

solution_variants.name: batched-histogram-binary-search
solution_variants.summary: Binary search the number of steps and test feasibility by greedily moving excess frequency mass in a histogram.

tags.evidence: The monotone maximum-frequency property allows binary search over the number of steps.
The check moves excess mass from the right greedily to the nearest available place or t positions forward.
The maximum frequency never increases under the process, making the decision predicate monotone.
